title: SMSFs back in from cold for wealth accumulation
full_text:
There is one winner in the wealth accumulation game out of Treasurer Jim Chalmers' fifth budget; the humble self-managed superannuation fund has once again become the preferred vehicle for Australians and their financial advisers.
Not long ago, they were on the outer. A new 15 per cent levy on earnings on all accounts with a balance of $3 million and over is barrelling towards the SMSF sector from July. Known as Division 296, the change prompted many to switch their assets to far more advantageous structures, such as a family trust.
But Tuesday night showed that they, too, are on borrowed time.
Distributions from discretionary trusts will be subject to a 30 per cent tax from mid-2028 under changes announced in the budget set to raise $4.47 billion. This is designed to end income splitting between beneficiaries, who will pay the rate on income from the trust, and "bucket companies", which will be double-hit by the distribution tax and the company rate.
David Busoli, principal of self-managed fund administration provider SMSF Alliance, said previous strategies designed for wealthy Australians to avoid the Division 296 impost now needed rethinking.
"The result is that the individual, including discretionary trust, investment alternative to superannuation has become less attractive," Busoli said in a note to subscribers on Thursday.
Meg Heffron, managing director of SMSF specialist firm Heffron, said it would be an interesting time for SMSFs given the government had "made everything outside of super less attractive". She predicted that people who were planning to change the structure of assets due to Division 296 would revert to superannuation.
Then there is the capital gains tax point.
Super funds - self-managed or otherwise - will remain exempt from Labor's capital gains tax changes, which from next July will repeal the 50 per cent discount and replace it with a less generous inflation-based offset and 30 per cent flat tax rate for all assets.
Jason Rademan, a partner at wealth management firm Lipman Burgon and Partners, said this meant assets held in super would retain an effective 10 per cent tax rate if the overall account balance was below the $3 million threshold for the new tax to kick in.
No CGT would be payable if a person was in the pension phase for super and their balance was below that cap, Rademan said
"Even with Division 296 applying above $3 million, superannuation remains the most tax-effective long-term structure for the vast majority of our clients," he said.
Despite the light-touch treatment in the budget, Heffron believed it was unlikely to accelerate the trend of people leaving the major super funds and setting up an SMSF.
Investors pulled a record $4.3 billion out of industry super funds in 2025 as members with high balances fled for better service in other funds or set up their own SMSF. Australian Tax Office data also indicates that self-managed funds are being created at the fastest pace on record, with 14,500 opened in the September quarter.
Heffron said the trend to SMSFs was "being driven demographically, rather than for policy reasons".
"The trend is largely because you get high-earning couples putting 12 per cent of their super away - they are stuffing their sock and looking at it as something far more meaningful than just a passive tool," he said.
"That isn't really the same cohort as the ones thinking about family trusts."
summary: Self-managed superannuation funds (SMSFs) are becoming popular again after changes in the budget that make other investment options less attractive. New taxes on trusts and capital gains mean SMSFs remain a tax-effective choice for many Australians.
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers' budget favours SMSFs for wealth accumulation.
A new 15% tax on earnings over $3 million will start in July.
Discretionary trusts will face a 30% tax from mid-2028.
Super funds remain exempt from capital gains tax changes.
Record withdrawals from industry super funds occurred in 2025.
14,500 new SMSFs were created in the September quarter.
ticker_line: SMSFs regain popularity amid new tax changes
